channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Iran-Egypt ‘Pride Match’ Stirs Conversation About Persecution
item.description: Seattle had designated Friday’s match to celebrate L.G.B.T.Q. rights, but the teams that faced off are from countries that penalize homosexuality.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans inside the stadium in Seattle holding up a Progress Pride flag during the Egypt and Iran match on Friday.

item.title: Dan Sullivan Can Join Dan Sullivan on Alaska Senate Ballot, Judge Says
item.description: The ruling reversed a decision by the state’s elections office that had found Dan J. Sullivan was ineligible to challenge Dan S. Sullivan, an incumbent senator.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Sullivan for Senate; Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Dan J. Sullivan, left, filed to challenge Senator Dan S. Sullivan, Republican of Alaska, for his seat, but his spot on the ballot has been contested.

item.title: U.S. Begins Investigating Polymarket, a Test of a Key Regulator
item.description: Last year, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission overruled its enforcement attorneys and killed a separate inquiry into whether the Trump-tied company was illegally serving U.S. customers.
item.dc:creator: Sharon LaFraniere and David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Associated Press
item.media:description: The Commodity Futures Trading Commission. has begun investigating Polymarket, a prediction market company tied to Donald Trump Jr.

item.title: Weinstein Will Be Resentenced in California Rape Case
item.description: The disgraced film producer had been given 16 years in prison in one of several prosecutions that have had him behind bars since 2020.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Adam Gray
item.media:description: The former Hollywood film producer Harvey Weinstein in court in Manhattan this month.

item.title: Convicted Rapist Who Fled to Scotland and Faked His Own Death Dies in Utah
item.description: Nicholas Rossi, 38, raped two girlfriends in 2008 and later fled to Scotland, prosecutors said. An attentive nurse treating him for Covid in 2021 identified him.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman
item.media:credit: Jeff J Mitchell/Getty Images
item.media:description: Nicholas Rossi, who fled the United States and was accused of faking his own death before being convicted of rape charges, died in prison.

item.title: Bodies of 117 Dogs Are Found at ‘No-Kill’ Sanctuary in California, Sheriff Says
item.description: Many of the dogs that were found buried at Miranda’s Rescue Animal Sanctuary in Fortuna, Calif., showed evidence of bullet fragments, investigators said.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker and Remy Tumin

item.title: Wildfire Incinerates a ‘Hidden Gem’ in Utah’s Mountains
item.description: The Cottonwood fire has grown to 72,000 acres and torn through more than 100 condos and cabins at a growing ski community.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy and Benjamin Wood
item.media:credit: Mike Mcmillan/U.S. Forest Service, via U.S. Forest Service, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Thursday’s conditions were more favorable for slowing the fire than those forecast for the weekend.

item.title: In Small Step, Israel Agrees to Withdrawal From Two Areas in Lebanon
item.description: Secretary of State Marco Rubio acknowledged that the agreement marked only “the beginning of the beginning” of negotiations between Israel and Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: David Guttenfelder/The New York Times
item.media:description: The remains of a destroyed street in Mayfadoun, Lebanon, on Monday following fighting between Israel and Hezbollah.

item.title: How the Reflecting Pool Turned Green: Missing ‘Bubblers’ and a Rush Job
item.description: Bulky “nanobubbler” machines were carted off ahead of a promotional event for President Trump’s Ultimate Fighting Championship birthday party.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Rahmat Gul/Associated Press

item.title: What to Know About Problems at the Reflecting Pool and Trump’s Claims of Vandalism
item.description: The administration has provided little evidence to back up some of his assertions about the Lincoln Memorial pool. Experts say other factors could have caused the major problems that have plagued it.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold, Maxine Joselow and Clarence Williams
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
item.media:description: A maintenance worker cleaned around the perimeter of the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool while National Guard troops patrolled on Friday.

item.title: Pete Buttigieg Says He Was Separated From His Children in ‘Swatting’ Attack
item.description: The former transportation secretary recounted being kept away from his 4-year-old twins overnight after an anonymous report accused him of posing a threat to them. The police said the report was false.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Thalassa Raasch for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pete Buttigieg, the former transportation secretary, is a prominent Democrat who is widely seen as a potential 2028 presidential candidate.

item.title: ‘Pride Match’ Celebration Proves Awkward for Iran-Egypt Game in Seattle
item.description: Although homosexuality is illegal in both countries, their World Cup game has been designated a “Pride Match” by Seattle’s organizing committee to mark the month celebrating the L.G.B.T.Q. community.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: David Ryder/Reuters
item.media:description: A truck outside the stadium in Seattle on Friday delivered a message in protest of the upcoming “Pride Match.”

item.title: Tackling the Politics of the World Cup
item.description: The soccer tournament has brought a flood of political story lines.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times

item.title: Bill Pulte Picks G.O.P. Election Operative for Spy Agency Job
item.description: Christina Norton is the new chief of staff at the Office of the Director of National Intelligence, former officials said.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes and Dustin Volz
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: Bill Pulte became the acting head of the department of national intelligence last week after President Trump named him to replace Tulsi Gabbard.

item.title: How the Supreme Court Removed Key Protections for Immigrants
item.description: Our Supreme Court reporter Ann E. Marimow describes how a 6-3 majority of the justices removed protections for immigrants and asylum seekers amid the Trump administration’s crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow, Gilad Thaler, Jon Miller, Nikolay Nikolov, Stephanie Swart, Rafaela Balster, Ray Whitehouse, Paul Abowd and Allison McCann

item.title: Centrist Democrats Rebuke Party’s Left Wing: ‘We Are Capitalist, Not Socialist’
item.description: Following high-profile primary victories by democratic socialists, some moderate Democrats are advocating a different course, highlighting tensions in the party.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: A letter, titled “The Promise to America,” from moderate House Democrats, was led by Representative Tom Suozzi, a New York Democrat who flipped a swing district on Long Island two years ago.

item.title: Earthquake Tests Growing Ties Between U.S. and Venezuela
item.description: The Trump administration said it would commit aid, at a time when it has been expanding U.S. commercial interests in Venezuela beyond oil.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong and Michael Crowley
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rescue workers and civilians looking for people trapped under debris in La Guaira, Venezuela, after two large earthquakes.

item.title: D.C. Reaches Settlement in Lawsuit Brought by ‘Star Wars’ National Guard Protester
item.description: Sam O’Hara was playing “Imperial March,” the theme of the film series villains, while protesting the presence of National Guard troops in the capital when he was handcuffed by city police officers.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of the National Guard at Union Station in Washington last September.

item.title: Yale Seeks Trump Administration Deal in Inquiry Over Race and Admissions
item.description: The university hired a high-powered law firm to try to reach an agreement with the Justice Department over claims its admissions practices hurt white and Asian applicants.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt, Alan Blinder and Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Christopher Capozziello for The New York Times
item.media:description: While a deal could alleviate federal pressure on Yale, it could create problems closer to campus. Schools that have cut deals with the Trump administration have faced a backlash from students, faculty and alumni.

item.title: Virginia Man Sentenced to 40 Years for Setting City Councilman on Fire
item.description: Shotsie Buck-Hayes severely burned J. Lee Vogler, a councilman in Danville, Va., last year in an attack that the police said stemmed from a “personal matter.”
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.media:credit: Allen G. Breed/Associated Press
item.media:description: Shotsie Buck-Hayes during a preliminary hearing for his case in Danville, Va., in September.

item.title: Prosecutors in Washington Say Citations Have Been Issued at Reflecting Pool
item.description: A spokesman provided no further details, saying those who received the citations were ordered to appear in court at a later date. No records are available.
item.dc:creator: Clarence Williams
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has blamed vandals for the myriad problems with the renovation of the reflecting pool in Washington.

item.title: Gracie the Giraffe Is Found ‘Fat and Happy’ 2 Weeks After Escaping Texas Ranch
item.description: Gracie caused a stir after she slipped away into the Texas Hill Country, but was spotted during a helicopter search on Friday. Now it’s time to bring her home.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather

item.title: N.M. Governor Asks for Criminal Investigation of D.E.A. Agents
item.description: New reports this week indicated that federal agents knowingly let fentanyl flood the streets of New Mexico as they pursued larger drug cases.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michelle Lujan Grisham in Santa Fe, N.M., in 2025.

item.title: Prosecutors in Kirk Case Found in Contempt for Media Statement
item.description: The judge in the Charlie Kirk murder trial declined defense requests to throw out the death penalty as punishment but did order prosecutors to pay some of the defense’s legal fees.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A makeshift memorial for Charlie Kirk in Orem, Utah, after he was killed at Utah Valley University last year.

item.title: Mistrial Declared in Palisades Fire Arson Case
item.description: A mistrial was declared in the arson case against Jonathan Rinderknecht, who was accused of setting a brush fire that later became the deadly Palisades fire.
item.dc:creator: Maia Spoto, Orlando Mayorquín and Vik Jolly
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Destroyed houses in a residential neighborhood after the Palisades fire last year.

item.title: Trump Administration to Investigate California’s Powerful Coastal Agency
item.description: Howard Lutnick, the commerce secretary, has accused the state of “environmental extremism” amid a federal push for oil production.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: The coastline in Big Sur, Calif., in April.

item.title: How Trump’s Political Agenda Is Shaped by His Own Obsessions
item.description: President Trump’s priorities seem increasingly detached from the concerns of voters and his party.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has been focused on pet projects like his expansive and costly renovations at the White House and around the nation’s capital.

item.title: Vance Downplays Watergate and Compares Himself to Nixon
item.description: The vice president said that the scandal that ended Richard Nixon’s presidency would be “like a 12-hour news story” if it happened today and that the “deep state” had taken down Nixon.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance in Maine last month. At an event on Thursday at the Richard Nixon Presidential Library and Museum, he compared himself to the former president: “Young senator, vice president, writes some best-selling books, is hated by the media.”

item.title: Georgia Delays Change to Its Vote-Counting System
item.description: A law banning QR codes on ballots had been set to go into effect on July 1. Lawmakers voted to push back its implementation until 2028.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Under the state’s current voting system, voters use touch-screen machines to make their ballot choices, which are then printed on a sheet of paper along with a QR code. Voters can review their choices on the printout, but a scanner counts their choices using the code.

item.title: John Bolton, Former Trump Adviser, Pleads Guilty in Classified Information Case
item.description: Mr. Bolton admitted to mishandling classified information and could face time in prison, in an inquiry that spanned the Trump and Biden administrations.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi and Devlin Barrett
item.category: The Room Where It Happened: A White House Memoir (Book)
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: John R. Bolton, who became one of President Trump’s most outspoken critics, arriving at Federal District Court in Greenbelt, Md., on Friday morning.

item.title: Supreme Court’s TPS Ruling Capped a Long Campaign by Immigration Hard-Liners
item.description: “This is a victory 10 years in the making,” a White House official said after the Supreme Court ruled that the Trump administration could end deportation protections for some migrants.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Stephen Miller, the White House deputy chief of staff, is an architect of some of President Trump’s most aggressive immigration policies,

item.title: Pro-MAGA Groups Spur Trump Push For Cultural Change In Schools
item.description: One nonprofit, Defending Education, initiated nearly a dozen civil rights investigations targeting diversity programs and transgender policies.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Nicole Neily, the president and founder of the conservative parents’ rights group Defending Education, near her home in Arlington, Va.

item.title: Freedom Con: Thousands of Men Gather for Christianity and Feats of Strength
item.description: Thousands of men and boys gathered in central Washington for masculinity, Christianity and right-wing politics.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Graham
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: The two-day Freedom Con was held over Father’s Day weekend.

item.title: How Trump’s Education Department Is Targeting Trans Students
item.description: Our Washington correspondent Michael Bender explains how the Trump administration is trying to use a civil rights law that prohibits sex-based discrimination to roll back protections for transgender students.
item.dc:creator: Michael C. Bender, Nikolay Nikolov, Paul Abowd, Jon Miller, Rafaela Balster, Stephanie Swart and Ray Whitehouse
